headline: A Dinosaur's Final Stand: Scientists Decode 66-Million-Year-Old Attack
meta_description: A fossilized Edmontosaurus skull with an embedded T. rex tooth reveals rare evidence of how the ancient predator hunted its prey face-to-face.
summary_short: Scientists studying a 66-million-year-old fossil at Montana State University have uncovered a dramatic predator-prey encounter frozen in time.
body:
Sometimes the most profound stories lie hidden in plain sight—preserved in stone for millions of years, waiting for curious minds to piece them together.

That's exactly what happened when researchers at Montana State University's Museum of the Rockies examined a duck-billed Edmontosaurus skull discovered in 2005 in eastern Montana's Hell Creek Formation. One detail made their hearts race: a broken Tyrannosaurus tooth still lodged in the animal's face.

"Although bite marks on bones are relatively common, finding an embedded tooth is extremely rare," explains doctoral student Taia Wyenberg-Henzler, who led the investigation with Museum curator John Scannella. "It gives you the identity of not only who was bitten but also who did the biting."

Using CT scans at a local hospital, the team reconstructed what happened roughly 66 million years ago near the end of the Age of Dinosaurs. The tooth's position in the Edmontosaurus's nose tells a striking story: the herbivore faced its attacker directly. The force required to break off the tooth suggests deadly power—a moment of terror that science has now made visible across eons.

The skull sits today in the Museum's Hall of Horns and Teeth, a window into a world we'll never see but can finally understand.
